chunks.title: Introduction to Diabetics and Honey
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the topic by discussing the effects of honey on diabetic patients and the general impact of artificial sweeteners, which are deemed harmful to insulin sensitivity. They emphasize the potential benefits of studying natural honey as an alternative.
chunks.transcript: let's talk about diabetics and honey specifically as a model of this diabetes and sugar suggestion that I'm making and we'll move from there but this is all framed in the conversation of artificial sweeteners good or bad I'm going to show you lots of interesting evidence in humans and animal studies in this podcast showing that they're harmful for you in many ways including worsening your insulin sensitivity so I don't think anyone should be eating these even people who are diabetic definitely if

chunks.title: Study Design and Intervention
chunks.summary: A randomized controlled trial involving 48 type 2 diabetic patients is described, focusing on the administration of natural honey in increasing amounts over eight weeks. The speaker details the specific amount of honey given to participants and the design of the study.
chunks.transcript: show you guys is this one it's an eight week randomized controlled clinical trial done in 2009 looking at the effects of natural honey consumption in diabetic patients these are humans all right this is 48 type 2 diabetic patients who were put into two groups they received oral natural honey for eight weeks and the control group that did not take honey so that is the only intervention they gave these people honey and no honey how much did they give them you can see here in the experimental design the first two weeks they got one gram per kilogram per day of Honey the second two weeks 1.5 grams per kilogram per day the third two weeks two grams per kilogram per day and the last two weeks 2.5 kilograms per day 2.5 grams per kilogram per day so I'm about 70 kilograms 75 kilograms about 165 170 pounds so at the end of this study somebody who's like me five nine moderately muscular probably would have different body composition as a diabetic but 170 pound person would be getting around 170 plus grams of honey per day at the end of the study that's a lot of Honey 170 grams of honey that's 10 11 tablespoons of honey per day these diabetics we're getting at the end of the study in the last two weeks at the beginning of the study a 70 kilogram diabetic is going to get 70 grams of honey and they're going to get 105 grams of honey then they're going to get 140 grams of honey per day and then at the end they're going to get 175 grams of honey per day 70 kilogram human so a lot of Honey being given to these people over eight weeks now I want to show you guys the results of

chunks.title: Results of the Honey Study
chunks.summary: The results reveal that the honey group improved their insulin sensitivity and lost weight, despite an increase in blood sugar. The speaker highlights the significance of the fasting blood sugar metric as an indicator of insulin sensitivity.
chunks.transcript: was that insulin sensitivity improved and lipids improved in the honey group now blood sugars went up I'll show you this in a moment hemoglobin A1c went up but fasting blood glucose the best metric of insulin sensitivity went down in these diabetics this was the only intervention they did these people didn't get rid of seed oils they didn't get rid of anything else that I think is a problem for diabetics they could have done a study way better if you actually wanted to reverse these people's diabetes give them an animal-based diet give them meat give them organs give them raw dairy get them into animal base 30 which is happening now go to animalbase30.com to sign up and join us at heart and soil if you want to do animal base 30 it's a free 30-day animal-based eating challenge we've had so many diabetics do this Challenge and they all lose weight and get significantly better so in eight weeks if you give me a diabetic person I can significantly improve their diabetes just by changing their diet and could even give them honey but in this trial none of that happened they did not do an animal-based diet they did not improve the quality of the diet all they did was give them honey in significant amounts and what happened they got more insulin sensitive they didn't get less insulin sensitive they became more insulin sensitive so you can see here in the honey group also lost weight they started at 71.3 kilograms plus or minus 12.7 and they ended the trial at 69.5 kilograms that's basically two kilograms in eight weeks so about four pounds or more than four and a half pounds over eight weeks and all I did was give them more honey they lost weight the control group did not lose any weight the exact same weight at the beginning so the honey group lost weight that was statistically

chunks.title: Discussion on Blood Sugar Metrics
chunks.summary: The speaker explains how the significant amount of honey consumed led to changes in blood sugar metrics, including hemoglobin A1c levels. They speculate on the long-term effects of improved insulin sensitivity in the honey group.

chunks.title: Contrasting Views on Sugars and Health
chunks.summary: The conclusion emphasizes the contradiction between conventional wisdom regarding sugars and the study’s findings, which suggest honey may not be detrimental for diabetics. The speaker urges listeners to consider broader health metrics beyond just blood sugar.
chunks.transcript: hemoglobin A1c went up because their blood sugar went up we know this is going to happen you can see here on these blood work results the triglycerides went from 190 to 148 in the honey group The HDL went from 59 to 66 the LDL went from 125 to 107. so all of these metrics got better in a honey group in diabetics so I think this flies in the face of the convention that sugar is horrible for diabetics the honey is horrible for diabetics you should never do this if you're completely myopic and you only think about your blood sugar which misses in fact the actual pathology the roots of diabetes you might see that as a problem but if you're looking at the causative things here if you're looking at the insulin sensitivity fasting blood sugar lipids as an indicator of insulin sensitivity you'll see very clearly that giving honey diabetics actually made them less insulin resistant caused them to lose weight lipids improved and improved their
